summary: Crash on Ubuntu 25.10 with Mesa NVK driver

description.text:
### Tested versions

- Reproducible in 4.4.1 4.5 4.5.1
- Did not crash in Ubuntu 24.04 with 4.4.1 or 4.5


### System information

Godot v4.5.1.stable - Ubuntu 25.10 25.10 on Wayland - X11 display driver, Multi-window, 3 monitors - Vulkan (Forward+) - dedicated NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1070 (NVK GP104) - AMD Ryzen 5 3600X 6-Core Processor (12 threads) - 15.05 GiB memory

### Issue description

Godot crashes after some time. I tried multiple different projects, including a new, completely empty project. 

There is no specific thing that triggers it. Sometimes happens in a scene, sometimes in project settings, or just when it idles in the background. Sometimes it crashes very quickly (<10 s), sometimes it takes 30 s or so.

summary: Godot 4.5.1 Buttons will not work across viewports

description.text:
### Tested versions

- Reproducible in 4.5.1 stable
- Not reproducible in 3.6 rc.1

Using compatibility mode for 4.5.1 and gles3 for 3.6

### System information

Windows 10 - Intel i5 4690k - gtx 1060 6gb

### Issue description

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/8fceb756-0423-4aae-b6e2-2aea87d2da41)

In godot 3, you could have multiple viewport containers, and buttons within each viewport
The buttons could function like this

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/28aac97c-122d-4909-866c-48d4a911f796


whereby you can click buttons as you'd expect you could despite the buttons originating from different viewport containers

However, in Godot 4.5.1, if you attempt this same structure, you can only click the button whose viewport container is in front of all the others; you cannot 'click' through a viewport container despite it appearing as if you could.

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/44dc0e46-3590-497c-9e49-2c6a114478b0


Unclear if this is a bug, or removed functionality.

Run the control node in both of these projects, and simply try to click both buttons. Only in Godot 3 can you click both.

It does not appear that changing mode (compatibility, forward plus) or changing how the window is run (embedded, floating) has any effect.

### Steps to reproduce

Godot 4
1. create two viewport containers with viewport children
2. set the viewports to have transparent backgrounds
3. put a button in each viewport
4. offset the second button so that it covers part of the first button but not the entirety
5. run the scene
6. try to click both buttons; you will find that you can only click the front most one
To recreate in Godot 3, simply follow all the same steps, but you will find that it does work

### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)

[Godot4ViewportError.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/23165695/Godot4ViewportError.zip)
[Godot3ViewportError.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/23165696/Godot3ViewportError.zip)

summary: Null Reference Error for Custom Nodes in Editor Mode

summary: Major FPS drop in same project after upgrading from Godot 4.4.1 to 4.5

description.text:
### Tested versions

Godot version:

4.4.1 (previous stable)

4.5 (stable)

### System information

Windows 10 (build 19045) - Multi-window, 1 monitor - OpenGL 3 (Compatibility) - Intel(R) UHD Graphics 620 (Intel Corporation; 31.0.101.2130) - Intel(R) Core(TM) i5-8250U CPU @ 1.60GHz (8 threads)

### Issue description

After updating from Godot 4.4.1 to 4.5, the same project and scene experience a significant FPS drop with no code or asset changes.
This occurs in a 3D battle scene that includes multiple animated characters, environmental objects, and lighting.

The battlefield contains 7 animated units with polygon counts ranging from 1,260 to 2,712 each.
Additional scene assets include trees and rocks with polygon counts ranging from 50 to 1,900.
No complex shaders, particles, or post-processing effects are used.
<html>
<body>
<!--StartFragment--><html><head></head><body>
Version | Scene State | Observed FPS

4.4.1 | Idle / Walk animation | 52–60 FPS
4.5 | Same scene, same setup | 22-23 FPS

</body></html><!--EndFragment-->
</body>
</html>

Both versions were tested on the same laptop, with no other applications running and identical project settings.
No profiler, debugger, or visual debugger was active during the tests.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Open the same project originally made in Godot 4.4.1.
2. Run the same battle scene directly.
3. Observe FPS values at idle and during basic animations.
4. Compare performance results between 4.4.1 and 4.5.

### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)

Expected result:

Performance should remain consistent or at least within a small margin between stable versions.

Actual result:

FPS drops from ~60 to ~22–23 in Godot 4.5 when running the same scene.

Minimal reproduction project:

A simplified version of the battle scene can be provided upon request for reproduction purposes.

summary: Failed to create framebuffer in Compositor Effect with MSAA

description.text:
### Tested versions

Reproducible in 4.4.1 and 4.5.1

### System information

Godot v4.5.1.stable - Ubuntu 24.04.2 LTS 24.04 on X11 - X11 display driver, Multi-window, 2 monitors - Vulkan (Forward+) - dedicated NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1080 Ti (nvidia; 535.274.02) - Intel(R) Core(TM) i7-6850K CPU @ 3.60GHz (12 threads) - 31.25 GiB memory

### Issue description

Background: I am working on an atmosphere sky and volumetric clouds with compositor effect and I would like to use this chance to explore the possibility of using Slang as shading language (because it provides a lot of modern language features that GLSL does not) (I manually compile Slang to SPIRV for Godot to use) . Slang works well and is not related to this issue, it is just the reason that I attempt to use `RenderingDevice` directly to draw meshes in compositor effect  (that is the only way to reuse my Slang code for computer shaders instead of rewriting it in GDShader). I have little knowledge of Vulkan and the only GDScript code I could find to draw meshes using RenderingDevice is [this one](https://zhuanlan.zhihu.com/p/20314165178)  (page in Chinese), so I just copied it.

It uses `FramebufferCacheRD.get_cache_multipass` with color and depth textures from `RenderData` to construct a framebuffer.
```gdscript
var tex_color := render_bufs.get_color_layer(0)
var tex_depth := render_bufs.get_depth_layer(0)
var frame_buffer := FramebufferCacheRD.get_cache_multipass([tex_color, tex_depth], [], 1)
```

The function works fine with MSAA off, but does not when MSAA is on.
```text
ERROR: Texture format for index (1) requires an attachment (color, depth-stencil, input or VRS) bit set.
ERROR: servers/rendering/renderer_rd/framebuffer_cache_rd.h:155 - Condition "rid.is_null()" is true. Returning: rid
```

I don't know if it is the ideal way to create a framebuffer in a compositor effect. Using `RenderingDevice.framebuffer_create_multipass` with the same parameters simply crashes the engine. And I don't see any existing framebuffer in `RenderData` that I can use directly.

It seems that compositor effect currently has a lot of issues. In addition to this one, writing color buffer makes no effect with MSAA on in all stages except for post-transparent, as already reported in #106743. Also, due to #80834, predeletion callback in GDScript does not really work so RIDs can not be properly freed, this I have a PR #101269 to fix but it has not been reviewed yet.



### Steps to reproduce

* Create a compositor effect and use the mentioned code to create a framebuffer. 
* Change the AA option to MSAA.

### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)

[compositor-msaa.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/23155544/compositor-msaa.zip)

Default is with MSAA off so in Editor you can see a box drawn in the scene through a compositor effect. But as you turn MSAA on the box disappears and the error messages start to spam

summary: Save project metadata less often

description.text:
Right now `set_project_metadata()` will always save the file. This is rather wasteful, toggling a checkbox will result in disk write.

This PR defers the saving until editor saves external resources (e.g. when saving a scene) or exits, so writing the file should be much less frequent.

summary: Convert Theme Overrides to Type Variation

description.text:
Closes https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/12714 .

> [!warning]
> This PR is *not* currently in a merge-able state. I feel the core functionality — moving the overrides to the type variation — needs to be reviewed, which is why I haven't marked it as a draft. However, there are still things that need to be worked out regarding its user interface. See the "To do" section below for more details.

This PR adds a button to the Control inspector, which pushes the Control's current theme overrides to the theme it is using as a type variation. More specifically:
- If the Control currently does not have a type variation, the user is prompted to enter a name for one. The type variation is created for the theme with the Control's overrides applied, the overrides are removed from the Control, and the type variation is applied to the Control:

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/d12e697f-44ad-4299-8d66-e4a210c71da1

- If the Control does have a type variation, the Control's overrides are applied to the type variation and removed from the Control itself:

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/fb0c6b61-cd9c-4c92-905d-478241cb4fa3

## To do
- [x] Ask the user for a name for the Type Variation, instead of using a hardcoded name.
- [x] If the Control already has a Type Variation, then update the Type Variation with those overrides.
- [x] Add undo/redo support.
- [ ] Immediately refresh the Inspector so that it shows the new Type Variation and lack of overrides.
- [ ] Figure out a better location for the button itself.
  - Place it as a tool button at the end of the "Theme Overrides" properties?
- [ ] Looks possible that the Theme itself is saving the changes to it, even if we don't save the project? Might need to investigate further
	- [ ] Try this:
		- Edit a label using overrides, then apply those overrides to its variation
		- **Without saving**, quit Godot
		- When you open the project, the label will be back to what it was before the overrides, but the variation will still have the overrides?
- [ ] Squash commits once everything seems good

summary: Rework scene preview thumbnails - take 2

description.text: Supersedes #102313 and apply related fixes: #107719 #107514 #107676 Addressed issue: #107764 And to address the performance problem mentioned at #107736, this PR adds following changes: 1. Runs all thumbnail creation on a separate thread, no more progress bars 2. Add an editor setting `docks/filesystem/thumbnail_file_size_threshold`. When a scene file (gltf, tscn, fbx) is queued for thumbnail creation, we first interpret the scene file with `FileAccess` directly, check for all external, internal resources it uses, and see if the accumulated size (in bytes) is larger than this threshold, if it is, we don't proceed to generate thumbnail for it, instead we give it a 2x2 dummy thumbnail. As scene files could be large levels, and multi-threaded scene loading still isn't possible now, for creating thumbnails, we have to target files that are likely prefabs and can be loaded instantly. * NOTE 1: I had tried counting tris and pixels for each resources, it turns out to be too slow which destroys the purpose of quick checking, and also very buggy due to `GLTFDocument` might be stateful. Estimating total file bytes seems to be the only viable option now. * NOTE 2: Dummy thumbnail is mandatory to prevent it being queued over again, as the thumbnail generator logic will think it's aborted then proceed to retry if there's no thumbnail present. * NOTE 3: Changing `docks/filesystem/thumbnail_file_size_threshold` won't re-queue all the files, you have to edit and re-save individually for it to update. Large levels would likely be ignored by thumbnail generator with the default setting -- **64MB**: 3. For stability reasons, `PackedScene::instantiate()` and `Node::queue_free()` calls are all deferred. The main caveat of this PR is that if some scene files have ~30MB worth of bytes, it will cause frame lags, but far from rendering the editor unresponsive, suppose this price will only be paid once on a freshly opened project, this should be worth it. Code could use some clean up, testers are welcome now. I've tested with official template projects, and also a project that has numerous 50MB~100MB gltf files with 4K HDRIs, all looks good to me now. ------ *2025-10-27-08:30 Update* If the code is stable and testing results are good, I plan to implement following changes by order, but no guarantees, just a list of nice-to-have if I got time. **TODO:** - [x] ~Add setting `docks/filesystem/thumbnail_max_scene_node_count`, ignore thumbnail creation if scene has too many nodes. (default 2000?)~ * ~This should account for scene files that are under file size threshold, but have a huge amount of instanced nodes sharing the same resources, which might take too much time to render in background and cause lags.~ * (2025-11-09) Edit : Discarded. Not possible to count nodes precisely without loading the files (scenes may contain gltf, fbx resources) - [x] Implement #102263 as a fallback for scene files that are too large to handle (with no options, just use fixed view) - [x] Bring back #96544 as a fallback for imported scene resources that are too large to handle

summary: Method doc tooltip miss indicate return enum value's class name(inconsistent with the method auto-completion tooltip)

description.text: ### Tested versions - Reproducible in: 4.5.1stable ### System information Godot v4.5.1.stable - Windows 10 (build 19045) - Multi-window, 1 monitor - Vulkan (Mobile) - dedicated NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4070 (NVIDIA; 32.0.15.7628) - 12th Gen Intel(R) Core(TM) i5-12490F (12 threads) - 31.81 GiB memory ### Issue description When using a method that returns an enumeration value, I found that it lacked a prompt for the class name to which the enumeration belongs, this will cause confusion when calling methods between classes have similar enumeration names. For example, if you have a Class named `Fruit` with an enumeration named `Type` representing the category of the fruit, and also you have anther Class named `Knife` with an enumeration named `Type` representing the category of the knife. Then, somewhere(e.g. a script which also has a `Type` enumeration) you have a function which return a fruit(or knife) instance's type(the example I gave may not be very appropriate, as you can always define a enumeration type variable named type for these classes and directly obtain the instance's category through access type variable). But, at this point, checking the document tooltips for those methods must confuse you(u can't distinguish the class enumeration Type belongs to, of course, you can always go to the definition by clicking on the enumeration name to view the class it belongs to, but this is not very intuitive). On the other hand, although you can solve this problem by always using different enumeration names, such as naming them `FruitType`(or `KnifeType`), this will increase the workload. But what I want to express is that when calling this function that returns an enumeration value, the documentation tooltip does not display the name of the class to which the enumeration belongs. It only simply displays something like `function_name ()-> Type` or `function_name_with_enum_para(para: Type) -> Type` **This behavior is inconsistent with the method auto-completion tooltip.** The following is the feature of the method auto-completion tooltips: - When you call those method using a script instance without class_name, the method auto-completion tooltip will display `script_name.gd enum_name function_name()` such as `my_node.gd.Type get_type()` - When you call those method using a script instance with class_name, the method auto-completion tooltip will display `class_name.gd enum_name function_name()` such as `MyNode.Type get_type()` - Also, when you call some method has enum parameters, method auto-completion will display the enum parameter's class name belongs to, like `MyNode.Type get_type_by_enum_para(para: MyNode.Type)` For the script where the enumeration definition is located, we can use enumeration names directly in function parameters or return values, omitting class name(This is reasonable because they are currently in the same class/domain, the code can be omitted), but whenever calling a function that using the enumeration value(whether as a parameter or a return value), I think the name of the enumeration class name belongs to must always be displayed in doc tooltips, just like the method auto-completion tooltip. It is reasonable to unify their behavior. #### Screenshots: **Attention: caller in the screenshot is always `my_node.gd`** _auto-completion tooltip examples(no omit class name, very clear):_ call self's function call other script's function _doc tooltip examples(no matter who the caller is, always omit class name, make confusion):_ call self's function call other script's function #### Demo: https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/94134eeb-b84b-4fba-a2ed-8022e9a738f5 ### Steps to reproduce N/A ### Minimal reproduction project (MRP) [issue_2025-10-27_00-39-50.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/23151267/issue_2025-10-27_00-39-50.zip)

summary: Missing `Default Texture Filter`  values

description.text: ### Tested versions All versions ### System information Irrelevant ### Issue description The `Default Texture Filter` in `Project Settings` only shows: And under Canvas Texture, it shows: And please, before you jump to the conclusion that this is intended, look at this: ### Windowed Mode 1280x720p ### Fullscreen Mode 1920x1080p Please compare the quality of each filtering option. The issue is that none of the default values of texture filter have good quality when viewed from distance. As you can see, Linear Mipmap blurs the image considerably to the point the wooden texture loses its details. Also, look at the grid lines on the old parchment. Linear Mipmap Anisotropic or Nearest Mipmap Anisotropic retains almost the same quality of the intended texture. While this texture is not on an angle (not oblique, but orthogonal to the screen). Mipmap Anisotropic was able retain the details of the textures. The blurriness with standard mipmapping is caused by a poor sampling pattern. The camera's zoom is changing the texture's size relative to the screen. The graphics card sees this as a form of non-uniform scaling, which anisotropic filtering is designed to handle. - Non-uniform sampling: Linear (and Nearest?) Mipmap Anisotropic filtering uses a more intelligent and directional sampling pattern when the texture is scaled unevenly. It samples multiple pixels along the direction of the scaling to preserve detail. - Mipmap Anisotropic Filtering accesses higher-resolution mipmaps, when the 2D texture is zoomed-out, standard mipmapping would choose a low-resolution mipmap and use a square sampling pattern. Anisotropic filtering, however, can sample from a higher-resolution mipmap to preserve detail and texture sharpness, even when the image is scaled down. So, please consider adding the missing 2 filtering options to the Default Texture Filter values. The reason for this, I have hundreds of nested nodes that have textures, sprites and whatnot. My game relies heavily on camera zoom levels and most importantly zooming out a lot. Default mipmap is very blurry and destroys the image quality greatly. Without adding these two filtering options, I have to manually adjust these nested texture nodes to have anisotropic filtering. Which is not ideal. ### Steps to reproduce apply texture filter from project and canvas texture filter ### Minimal reproduction project (MRP) none

summary: Boolean variable showing GDScriptFunctionState in some cases

description.text:
### Tested versions

Reproducable in v4.5.stable.official [876b29033], v4.4.1.stable.official [49a5bc7b6]


### System information

Godot v4.5.stable - Windows 11 (build 26200) - Multi-window, 2 monitors - Vulkan (Forward+) - dedicated NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1080 (NVIDIA; 32.0.15.7216) - 13th Gen Intel(R) Core(TM) i5-13600K (20 threads) - 31.77 GiB memory

### Issue description

The bug is that in a specific constellation a function that returns a boolean is a GDScriptFunctionState instead of a boolean.
```gdscript
var abstract: BaseClass = InheritedClass.new()

var res_v := await abstract.get_bool()
print(res_v == true)
print(res_v == true)
print(res_v == true)
print(res_v == true)
```
These prints in my example give the following output:

    <GDScriptFunctionState#-9223372000599210596>
    false
    <GDScriptFunctionState#-9223372000599210596>
    false
`print(res_v)` for example always returns the correct value, `print(not res_v)` returns the GDScriptFunctionState.
The problem occurs when these things come together:
1. a base class with a function that is not async.
2. an inherited class with the inherited function that is async and awaits a signal
3. some way to emit the signal (for example awaiting for a timer timeout didn't reproduce the bug)

It works only after casting to a bool like this:
`var result: bool = bool(res_v)`
Also the inheritance is important for the function. When one of these steps is done the problem disappears:
- `var abstract: BaseClass = InheritedClass.new()` changed to `var abstract: InheritedClass= InheritedClass.new()`
- add an `await` to the BaseClass to make the base function async (this is not possible if the function is abstract which was the case when the problem originally occured)

### Steps to reproduce

I added a GitHub link to a minimal project to reproduce the bug.
It contains two buttons. One to start a function to await a signal and the other one to emit the signal. When the first one is pressed and then the second one, you should see the output from above in the logs with the GDScriptFunctionState.

### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)

https://github.com/mLampir/Godot-Bug

summary: EditorPlugin._Build() base implementation returns 'false' (Godot C#)

description.text:
### Tested versions

-Reproducible in: 4.3.0, 4.4.1, 4.5.1

### System information

Windows 11 (build 26100) - Multi-window, 2 monitors - OpenGL 3 (Compatibility) - NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3060 (NVIDIA; 32.0.15.6094) - AMD Ryzen 9 7950X 16-Core Processor (32 threads)

### Issue description

 A standard practice for C# is to call the underlying implementation of a virtual method that is not abstract, so as to ensure that you are extending, not overriding, functionality unless you intend to do so explicitly, and that doing so will result in the same behavior as the base class. 

The EditorPlugin script virtual function _Build()'s base implementation returns 'false', which means 'build failed for plugin'.

Because of this, and because of how Godot hooks into Godot-C# crossover objects like Nodes, despite the following being by-standard identical to not overriding the method at all, it will cause the build to fail in the editor:
`public override bool _Build() 
{ 
base._Build();
}
`
Just by overriding the function then calling the base implementation, the class has reversed it's behavior for the function.


The result is very surprising and runs counter to language and convention standards. The base class should return 'true' to avoid such a scenario and maintain correctness by programming conventions.

### Steps to reproduce

-Create an EditorPlugin C# class & plugin file
-Override _Build() and add 'base._Build();
-Enable the plugin
-Breakpoint on base.Build()
-Click 'Run Project' and observe that the value returned is 'false'. The build will fail.
-Optionally, remove the override and then observe that the build succeeds.

### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)

N/A (any C# plugin will work)

summary: _Build() is not called on Build Project (Godot C#)

description.text:
### Tested versions

### Issue description

The EditorPlugin script function _Build() is not called when 'Build Project' is performed. It IS called, however, when 'Run Project' is performed. The behavior is very surprising and unexpected. It also leaves C# plugins without a reliable or practical way to clean up/shut down things gracefully without using reflection to bind to the GodotEditor build events.

If this is very intentionally the behavior for _Build(), then another method should be made available which does hook into Build Project, and _Build()'s documentation should be clarified.

### Steps to reproduce

-Create an EditorPlugin C# class & plugin file
-Override _Build() and add any content
-Enable the plugin
-Breakpoint on said content
-Click 'Build Project' in the editor, and observe that the breakpoint is not hit

summary: [DRAFT] XR Editor: Proof-of-concept immersive editing using the debugger

description.text:
In the Godot XR editor running standalone on the Meta Quest headset, you can launch your game immersively, and then bring the 2D editor window back up, and make edits to the current scene which will be immediately reflected in the immersive environment.

This is [great](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6RE8KuCspqw)! It's super helpful for doing level design, so you can see exactly how something will look in VR, especially when precise spacing is important.

However, even though the objects you're manipulating may be directly in front of you in the immersive environment, you still need to use the editor in the 2D panel to manipulate them.

Wouldn't it be great if you could just grab the objects in the immersive environment and move them around?

This PR is a proof-of-concept which shows how that could be implemented :-)

**Check out a quick video demo here:**

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=S_VliP5-3OU

**How to use it:**

- You press the "Menu" button to switch into "immersive editing mode", which puts a ray in your right hand and a flat panel in your left hand
- You can point the ray at an object in the scene, and press the trigger to select it - it'll get a selection box around it
- You can then press and hold the grip button, and move the controller to move the object immersively
- If the object you selected is from the current scene that's open in the editor, once you release the grip button, it'll update that object in the scene in the editor. If it's where you want it, you can just save those changes! It does its updates using the normal undo/redo system, so you can easily undo it in the editor too, if you want

_NOTE: The idea I had for the flat panel in your left hand, is that maybe it could show the inspector with the properties of the selected object and you could do some light editing there, but it's not implemented and I don't know if that's even a good idea._

**How this works:**

- It's using the debugger protocol to do the selection and send the edits back to the editor. This is very similar to how the current game view works, where you can select objects in the game view, and then it selects them in the editor. I think this overall approach is a good way to do this, and so is the main focus of the PoC. (Of course, the specific implementation of this approach is very early.)
- It's doing some unholy things with `XROrigin3D` and other XR nodes to enable/disable the immersive editing mode, and doesn't take the OpenXR Action Map into account at all. This will probably need to be completely redone in a real implementation - please don't pay too much attention to this part :-)

**Some things I'd like to have in the finished implementation:**

- Objects selected in immersive editing mode should show the same object selected in the scene in the editor
- A 3D transform gizmo that can be used to only translate or rotate on a specific axis, for fine-tuned editing. (It still makes sense to have a mode to just grab and freely move the object, but just not as the only option)
- The ability to launch a single scene, and have it inject the `XROrigin3D` and related nodes, and initialize OpenXR. Maybe this could even start in immersive editing mode? This way you could directly edit a specific scene, rather than launching the full game and having to find your way to the scene you want to edit
- Some form of locomotion that isn't connected with the game. Sometimes you may want to edit something that you can't physically reach, or the game's locomotion won't let you move to
- The ability to scale the immersive environment. When doing level design, it can be very useful to "zoom out" or "zoom in", and in an immersive context, I think that means scaling the world! Although, this would need to actually be tested to see just how useful it is
- The ability to place a cursor in the immersive environment, which can be used as a position to spawn a new node from the editor. This way you can create new things near yourself without having manually move them there in the 2D panel 
- Proper integration with the OpenXR Action Map. I think a special Action Set should be injected when the game is launched with immersive editing enabled (and this code should only exist in editor builds) and these actions should be used rather than the default Godot ones

Not all those things need to be in one big PR though :-)

summary: PhysicalBone3D gradually shift over time in editor

description.text:
### Tested versions

v4.5.stable.official [876b29033]
(v4.4 and 4.4.1 too if i remember correctly)

### System information

Windows 11

### Issue description

When leaving a scene containing a Skeleton3D character with generated PhysicalBone3D open in the editor for longer periods (a few hours), some PhysicalBone3D slowly drift from their original positions.

In my case, the drift mainly affects arms and fingers.
The drift direction isn't consistent, but it does consistently happen.

The editor doesn't flag the changes, but if I manually save, the distorted positions are written to the scene.
Closing without saving reverts to the original positions.

### Steps to reproduce

1. In the editor, import the .fbx character (idle.fbx from mixamo) 
2. Place it on the scene 
3. Make the node local to access the Skeleton3D
4. Create the Physical Skeleton
5. Switch to Display Overdraw in the view port to see the CollisionShape3D easier.
6. Leave the scene open in the editor for a few hours (~5 hours in the attached video).

After a while you may notice some CollisionShape3D have moved. Not the shapes themselves, but rather their parent PhysicalBone3D, as their transform.position will have changed.

### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)

You'll find attached the MRP consisting of two scenes and and the idle.fbx mixamo character :
- test_scene_new : contains freshly generated PhysicalBones3D
- test_scene_old : contains "old" PhysicalBones3D (aged roughly an hour)

(I would recommend starting from scratch as the scene takes 30 seconds to set up)

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/4df8d5f6-6054-42ee-94f8-a9275d10fd4c

You can clearly see the CollisionShape3D moving over time. But the actual culprit here is the parent PhyscialBone3D's transform.position which does slightly change over time. (unfortunately i didn't capture the transform in the time-lapse, sorry about that)

summary: Statically protect `Object::cast_to` for unrelated `Object` types.

description.text:
Introduces static protection to `Object::cast_to`, in the same way that `static_cast` is protected against casting unrelated types together.
This uncovers a handful of bugs that could have been avoided with this. It will also protect us against new bugs going forwards.
## Bug fixes

Here's the (necessary) fixes in this PR:

- `AudioStreamPolyphonic` currently doesn't pass the stream in to stop as expected (passing `nullptr` instead). I changed it to pass the object that was tested for. I'm not sure if my fix is correct, so review required by @godotengine/audio
- `GLTFDocument` attempted to cast `Image` to `Texture2D`, which is impossible. I've removed the casts. If convenience conversion is desired, this should be re-implemented correctly afterwards. cc @aaronfranke 
- `SceneImportSettingsDialog::_skeleton_tree_entered` did not set the skin as expected; instead, it currently sets it to `null`. I fixed it in the way that (i think) it was intended.
- The rest of the bugs were inconsequential, since a `nullptr` `Ref` in one type is the same as a `nullptr` ref in another.
- The `PopupMenu::_activate_submenu` change is a really weird one. It casts from `PopupMenu` to `PopupMenu`, and if it doesn't work (which it always does), it returns after calling `popup()`. I think removing this whole logic (which might be leftover after refactors) shouldn't change behavior, because the `if` will never be entered, and `submenu_pum` is equivalent to `submenu_popup`.
